Quantum computing makes waves in drug discovery
===============================================

[Alex Generous, PhD](https://www.stjude.org/research/progress/author/alexgenerous.html) • April 3, 2025 

![Image 8: Christoph Gorgulla, PhD and two other men talking in front of a computer screen](https://stjude.scene7.com/is/image/stjude/8260208713-028?wid=500)

Co-corresponding author Christoph Gorgulla, PhD, Center of Excellence for Data-Driven Discovery, Department of Structural Biology.

Many of us have been mesmerized by the simple elegance of waves flowing on the water’s surface. Whether at a beach, on a lake, or watching the rain fall into a pond, seeing the ripples intersect, interfere, and separate can feel meditative. These simple waves, both peaceful and powerful, govern the universe at the quantum scale (think even smaller than atoms). By harnessing the behavior of these waves at the quantum level, scientists are gaining a deeper understanding of molecules and proteins, which can significantly accelerate drug discovery.

The drug discovery process is an expensive and time-consuming endeavor, often costing billions of dollars and taking decades for a single drug to reach the market. However, these molecules have the potential to save and extend lives, galvanizing researchers, companies, and governments to seek ways to address the speed and expense of finding new therapeutics.

Machine learning is a computational technique that allows computer algorithms to learn from previous data and make predictions and decisions or to generate new data. This technology, often using generative artificial intelligence, is the foundation for programs such as ChatGPT. In drug discovery, machine learning can significantly speed up this process by analyzing vast amounts of data to identify and generate new potential drug candidates.

Recently, researchers at St. Jude and the University of Toronto showed that quantum computing, a technology that exploits quantum effects (such as superposition, entanglement, and interference), could boost machine learning-based drug discovery to find better molecules faster, including for previously “undruggable” targets. The research, published in [Nature Biotechnology](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39843581/), sets the stage for a future that leverages quantum research.

“This is the first time quantum computing has been successfully used for a drug discovery project that includes experimental validation,” said co-corresponding author Christoph Gorgulla, PhD, [Center of Excellence for Data-Driven Discovery](https://www.stjude.org/research/departments/structural-biology/center-of-excellence-data-driven-discovery.html), [Department of Structural Biology](https://www.stjude.org/research/departments/structural-biology.html). “By augmenting our machine learning model with quantum computing, we outperformed similar, purely classical [computing] machine learning models in identifying promising therapeutic compounds.”

Scientists targeted the KRAS (Kirsten rat sarcoma virus oncogene homolog) protein to showcase quantum computing's potential for drug discovery. KRAS is one of the most mutated genes in cancers and is known to be a difficult target, often being called “undruggable.” The researchers used a classical computer to input a database of all molecules experimentally confirmed to bind to KRAS and trained a machine-learning model with this data. They also included over 100,000 theoretical KRAS binders obtained from an ultra-large virtual screen.

After running the classical model, they fed the results into a filter/reward function that evaluated the quality of the generated molecules, allowing only those of sufficient quality to pass the filter. Afterward, they trained a quantum machine-learning model and combined it with the classical model to improve the quality of the generated molecules. They then cycled back and forth between training the classical and quantum models to optimize them in concert. Once completed, the models generated multiple novel molecules, called ligands, which were predicted to bind KRAS.

“We identified ligands for one of the most important cancer drug targets,” Gorgulla said. “We then validated our discoveries through experiments, finding two molecules with real-world potential for future evaluation. This study serves as proof-of-principle that quantum computing has the potential to enhance drug discovery greatly in the future.”

Quantum computing overcomes the limits of chemical calculation with waves
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

Chemistry is, by its nature, the interaction of molecules at the subatomic level (in particular, the electron level). These interactions are best described by quantum mechanics, the branch of physics concerned with the strange and complex world of the very small. Quantum computers are designed based on these same principles, making them better suited for analyzing the interactions of molecules over classical computers.

“Classical computers struggle to efficiently handle the thermodynamical and quantum mechanical calculations required to simulate a protein and all molecules that could potentially bind to it,” Gorgulla said. “But quantum computers can, in theory, be exponentially faster for these calculations, as they are built using these same principles and are therefore better equipped to simulate molecular systems on the quantum mechanical level.”

While classical computers can, in principle, perform these simulations, the computational cost quickly becomes impractical — especially when using highly accurate quantum mechanical methods, which can be crucial for modeling molecular interactions with high precision.

“Classical computing uses bits, which are either zero or one, like a light switch, to perform operations in a stepwise arithmetic process. It’s like adding one plus one to get two, then adding another one to get three. While this might seem quick for simple calculations, it becomes very time-consuming as the calculations become more complex,” Gorgulla explained. “Quantum computing is different — it uses qubits. Qubits can be zero, one, or even zero and one at the same time. Instead of a switch, qubits can be pictured as a sphere like Earth. The north and south poles corresponded to zero and one, but the qubit’s value could be anywhere on the surface, effectively in an infinite number of positions, allowing the computer to explore multiple solutions simultaneously.”

The overlapping state of being both one and zero is called superposition. So, instead of a specific value, a qubit represents multiple states between one and zero. The real power of quantum computing comes from using qubits in superposition that are also “entangled.” Entangled qubits act together in a coordinated way, losing their individual identities and always acting and influencing each other.

Unlike classical computers that perform arithmetic operations, entangled qubits perform operations that explore computations as waves. Just like waves on the ocean’s surface, these waves can interfere with each other, getting bigger (positive interference) or smaller (negative interference). The resulting waves represent a range of potential solutions to a problem rather than just one answer, all from a single run. The concepts of superposition, entanglement, and interference are used in the quantum simulation approach mentioned earlier, but also in other types of algorithms, such as quantum machine learning algorithms.

In the study’s drug discovery pipeline, the concepts of entanglement and interference were crucial for improving the accuracy of their machine learning model in predicting whether compounds could bind to the chosen target, in this case, KRAS. “We found a few potential molecules for the most common KRAS mutants for which there are currently no drugs on the market,” Gorgulla said. “But we hope this is just the first of the many targets for which we will discover and validate viable lead compounds using this technology.”

Bringing quantum computing’s waves to St. Jude
----------------------------------------------

Gorgulla was recently appointed as an Assistant Member in the Center of Excellence for Data-Driven Discovery, Department of Structural Biology. One of his objectives is to develop new computational methods in his research group and the other is to help co-lead the institution’s use of quantum computing in the biomedical sciences as part of the Office of Data Science together with [M. Madan Babu, PhD](https://www.stjude.org/research/labs/babu-lab.html), Senior Vice President for Data Science, Chief Data Scientist, and Director of the [Center of Excellence for Data-Driven Discovery](https://www.stjude.org/research/departments/structural-biology/center-of-excellence-data-driven-discovery.html) and [J. Paul Taylor](https://www.stjude.org/research/labs/taylor-lab.html), MD, PhD, St. Jude Executive Vice President and Scientific Director.

“My lab will focus on developing next-generation methods for ligand and drug discovery, using quantum computing and machine learning to unify accuracy and speed when exploring the vast chemical space for ligand discovery,” Gorgulla explained. “It’s a natural fit for the Center of Excellence for Data Driven Discovery within the Structural Biology Department, as they continue to integrate large datasets and unravel protein structures and unique conformations they adopt. Our work can take the next important step of identifying ligands and develop them into tool compounds to initiate drug discovery efforts.”

Gorgulla began his work at St. Jude in 2023 as part of the [Blue Sky for Seeing the Invisible in Protein Kinases](https://www.stjude.org/research/departments/structural-biology/blue-sky-initiative-protein-kinases.html), led by [Charalampos Kalodimos, PhD](https://www.stjude.org/research/labs/kalodimos-lab.html), [Structural Biology](https://www.stjude.org/research/departments/structural-biology.html) chair. There, he built a ligand discovery team that works on structure-based ligand discovery projects, looking at the conformation states (shapes) of protein kinases, the second most targeted proteins by pharmaceutical drugs. Through his work, he built research relationships and collaborated with colleagues, which paved the way for him to establish his own lab dedicated to transforming ligand discovery by harnessing quantum computing and machine learning.

“St. Jude has been an amazing fit for me scientifically,” Gorgulla concluded, “I’m happy to already be able to present early fruits of quantum computing’s application and hope to drive this wave of technology forward from the virtual and theoretical space to making a real-world difference for our patients.”

# Drug design on quantum computers

[*Nature Physics*](/nphys)
**volume 20**, pages 549–557 (2024)[Cite this article](#citeas)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The promised industrial applications of quantum computers often rest on their anticipated ability to perform accurate, efficient quantum chemical calculations. Computational drug discovery relies on accurate predictions of how candidate drugs interact with their targets in a cellular environment involving several thousands of atoms at finite temperatures. Although quantum computers are still far from being used as daily tools in the pharmaceutical industry, here we explore the challenges and opportunities of applying quantum computers to drug design. We discuss where these could transform industrial research and identify the substantial further developments needed to reach this goal.

How quantum computing is revolutionising drug development
=========================================================

[![Image 14: Quantum computing concept](https://www.ddw-online.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Shutterstock_2258502663-2.jpg)](https://www.ddw-online.com/how-quantum-computing-is-revolutionising-drug-development-34423-202504/)

_**Saurabh Singal**, Founder of Molecule AI, examines the impact that quantum computing can, and will have, on drug discovery and development._

### A quantum leap in pharma

In 2019, Google announced that its quantum computer had achieved a groundbreaking milestone 1 – solving a problem in 200 seconds that would take the most powerful supercomputers 10,000 years to complete. Dubbed “quantum supremacy,” this achievement hinted at the revolutionary potential of quantum computing (QC). For industries like pharmaceuticals, where the stakes are measured in human lives and billions of dollars, the promise of QC is nothing short of transformative.

Another dramatic instance of QC’s power comes from the pharmaceutical sector itself. In 2023, Forschungszentrum Jülich and Lund University researchers utilised D-Wave’s quantum annealer to address the protein folding problem 2. This breakthrough accelerated the discovery of a promising drug candidate and showcased how QC could unlock mysteries previously deemed unsolvable.

Adding to these stories, IBM, in collaboration with MIT researchers, tackled one of the toughest challenges in quantum chemistry – calculating the ground-state energy of complex molecules 3. They demonstrated that quantum computers could simulate the electronic structure of molecules with an accuracy unattainable by classical methods. This achievement paves the way for designing novel compounds in record time.

### Understanding the quantum advantage

Unlike classical computers, which rely on binary bits representing 0s and 1s, quantum computers operate using quantum bits or qubits. These qubits leverage two key principles of quantum mechanics: superposition 4 and entanglement 5.

**Superposition** allows qubits to exist in multiple states simultaneously, exponentially increasing the computational power of quantum systems. For example, while a classical bit can be 0 or 1, a qubit can represent 0, 1, or any combination of these states simultaneously.

**Entanglement** enables qubits to be interconnected in such a way that the state of one qubit is directly related to the state of another, regardless of distance. This property allows quantum computers to perform complex calculations in parallel rather than sequentially, as classical computers do.

The foundation of a quantum computer is its quantum processor, also known as a quantum processing unit (QPU). The QPU consists of an array of qubits interconnected via quantum gates, forming a quantum circuit that enables computations. This setup allows quantum computers to tackle problems considered intractable for classical machines.

### The promise of quantum computing in drug development

Drug development is arduous, with over 90% of drug candidates failing during clinical trials. Quantum computing offers a paradigm shift by addressing key challenges in the pharmaceutical R&D pipeline.

#### 1. Molecular modelling and simulations

Traditional molecular simulations often rely on approximations due to the computational limits of classical computers. This can lead to inaccuracies in predicting how molecules behave and interact with biological systems. Quantum computers, however, can simulate quantum systems –such as interacting molecules – directly and with unparalleled accuracy.

*   **Protein folding:** Predicting protein structures and their folding mechanisms is a notoriously complex problem. QC enables precise modelling of these processes, aiding in the identification of druggable targets.
*   **Drug-protein interactions:** By simulating how drug candidates interact with biologically relevant proteins, quantum computers can identify potential therapeutic compounds more effectively.

#### 2. Accelerating drug discovery

Quantum machine learning algorithms offer insights into molecular behaviour that surpass classical machine learning models. They enhance the accuracy of predictions for biological activity, toxicity, and efficacy, speeding up the identification of promising drug candidates.

For instance, predicting RNA structures – a critical task in nucleic acid drug discovery – has become more efficient with quantum technologies. Imperial College London and Zhejiang Lab researchers have used quantum processors to advance molecular docking and RNA folding predictions 6.

#### 3. Optimising clinical trials

Quantum computing’s ability to analyse vast datasets rapidly makes it invaluable for clinical trial optimisation:

*   **Patient stratification:** Identifying suitable patient groups based on genetic and demographic data.
*   **Trial site selection:**Optimising site locations to reduce costs and improve trial efficiency.
*   **Causality analysis:** Understanding the root causes of side effects through advanced modelling, enabling better safety surveillance.

### Real-world applications and collaborations

The pharmaceutical industry has already embraced QC, with several notable collaborations highlighting its potential:

*   **Pfizer and Gero****7,8****:** Using hybrid quantum-classical architectures for therapeutic target discovery in fibrotic diseases.
*   **Bayer and Google****9****:** Leveraging quantum chemistry to predict molecular properties at the atomic level.
*   **Pasqal and Qubit Pharmaceuticals****10****:** Developing quantum-classical approaches to analyse protein hydration.
*   **Cleveland Clinic and IBM****11,12****:** Established the first quantum computer dedicated to healthcare research.

Furthermore, companies like Merck 13, Roche, and Johnson & Johnson are driving innovation, with a 70% surge in QC-related patent filings reported in recent years.

### The challenges ahead

Despite its promise, the road to widespread adoption of QC in pharmaceuticals is fraught with challenges. The fact that quantum computers are still in their infancy means that error rates in quantum gates and limited qubit scalability are significant hurdles. There is also a shortage of talent due to the highly specialised knowledge required for QC. Pharma companies already struggle to attract digital talent for less niche technologies, making QC expertise even harder to secure. Lastly, the high cost of quantum systems limits access for smaller players in the pharmaceutical sector.

### The future of quantum computing in pharma

Industry experts predict that global pharmaceutical spending on QC will reach billions by 2030. The most significant impact is expected in the preclinical and clinical stages, where failure rates are highest. By reducing errors and increasing efficiency, QC could drastically lower costs and accelerate timelines.

### Broader implications

Beyond R&D, QC may influence other aspects of the pharmaceutical value chain:

*   **Regulatory approval:** QC can streamline the submission process to regulatory bodies like the FDA by providing more accurate simulations and reducing error rates.
*   **Supply chain optimisation:** Managing complex logistics for drug distribution could benefit from QC’s advanced computational capabilities.

### Conclusion

Quantum computing is not just a technological evolution; it’s a revolution. While significant challenges remain, the progress made by industry leaders and research institutions underscores QC’s vast potential. By addressing inefficiencies in drug development and clinical trials, QC promises to save time, reduce costs, and improve patient outcomes.

The pharmaceutical industry stands at the cusp of a quantum leap. The question is no longer whether quantum computing will transform drug development, but how quickly it will become an integral part of the process. For companies willing to invest in the groundwork today, the rewards could redefine the future of medicine.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Quantum computing allows for precise simulations of molecular structures and interactions, surpassing classical methods and improving understanding of complex biochemical processes.
By leveraging quantum algorithms, drug discovery timelines can be significantly reduced, accelerating the identification and validation of effective therapeutic candidates.
Quantum computing enhances the modeling of molecular interactions at an atomic level, providing insights into binding affinities and reaction mechanisms essential for drug design.
With quantum-enhanced machine learning, researchers can efficiently sift through vast chemical libraries to identify promising drug candidates for further study and development.
The integration of quantum computing into drug discovery has the potential to transform healthcare by producing safer, more effective drugs at lower costs and in shorter timeframes.
